From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Defense position in baseball and softball

For the album, see [Shortstop (album)](/wiki/Shortstop_(album) "Shortstop (album)").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4a/Baseball_SS.svg/250px-Baseball_SS.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Baseball_SS.svg)

The position of the shortstop

**Shortstop**, abbreviated **SS**, is the [baseball or softball fielding position](/wiki/Baseball_positions "Baseball positions") between [second](/wiki/Second_base "Second base") and [third base](/wiki/Third_base "Third base"), which is considered to be among the [most demanding](/wiki/Defensive_spectrum "Defensive spectrum") defensive positions. Historically, the position was assigned to defensive specialists who were typically poor at batting and were often placed at the bottom of the [batting order](/wiki/Batting_order_(baseball) "Batting order (baseball)"). Today, shortstops are often able to hit well and many are placed at the top of the lineup. In the [numbering system](/wiki/Baseball_positions "Baseball positions") used by [scorers](/wiki/Baseball_scorekeeping "Baseball scorekeeping") to record defensive plays, the shortstop is assigned the number 6.

More hit balls go to the shortstop than to any other position, as there are more [right-handed](/wiki/Right-handedness "Right-handedness") hitters in baseball than [left-handed](/wiki/Left-handedness "Left-handedness") hitters, and most hitters have a tendency to [pull the ball](/wiki/Pull_hitter "Pull hitter") slightly. Like a [second baseman](/wiki/Second_baseman "Second baseman"), a shortstop must be agile, for example when performing a [4-6-3 double play](/wiki/Glossary_of_baseball_terms#4-6-3_double_play "Glossary of baseball terms"). Also, like a [third baseman](/wiki/Third_baseman "Third baseman"), the shortstop fields balls hit to the left side of the [infield](/wiki/Infield#Baseball "Infield"), where a strong arm is needed to [throw out](/wiki/Out_(baseball) "Out (baseball)") a [batter-runner](/wiki/Baserunning#The_batter-runner "Baserunning") before they reach the safety of first base.

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Shortstop&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

[Doc Adams](/wiki/Doc_Adams "Doc Adams") of the [Knickerbockers](/wiki/Knickerbocker_Base_Ball_Club_of_New_York "Knickerbocker Base Ball Club of New York") created the concept of the shortstop position, according to baseball historian [John Thorn](/wiki/John_Thorn_(baseball_historian) "John Thorn (baseball historian)") and [Baseball Hall of Fame](/wiki/National_Baseball_Hall_of_Fame_and_Museum "National Baseball Hall of Fame and Museum") researcher Freddy Berowski.[[1]](#cite_note-SABR-1)[[2]](#cite_note-invent-2) In the first five years the Knickerbockers played, the team fielded anywhere from eight to eleven players. The only infielders were the players covering each of the bases; if there were more than eight players, extra [outfielders](/wiki/Outfielder "Outfielder") were sometimes used. The outfielders had difficulty throwing baseballs into the infield because of the balls' light weight. Adams's shortstop position, which he started playing at some time from 1849 to 1850, was used to field throws from the outfielders and throw to the three infielders.[[1]](#cite_note-SABR-1)[[3]](#cite_note-3) The position, more of a fourth outfielder than an infielder, was also then called "short fielder" (a term still used in soft-pitch softball for the 10th player).[[4]](#cite_note-4)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/83/Honus_Wagner_Pittsburgh.jpg/250px-Honus_Wagner_Pittsburgh.jpg)](/wiki/File:Honus_Wagner_Pittsburgh.jpg)

Honus Wagner

With the advent of higher-quality baseballs, Adams moved to the infield, since the distance the balls could travel increased.[[1]](#cite_note-SABR-1) However, [Dickey Pearce](/wiki/Dickey_Pearce "Dickey Pearce"), primarily of the [Brooklyn Atlantics](/wiki/Brooklyn_Atlantics "Brooklyn Atlantics"), is credited as the first to have played the shortstop position as it is played now.[[5]](#cite_note-5) Adams had a long playing career with the Knickerbockers: he remained a player with the team until 1860.[[6]](#cite_note-6)

## Positioning

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Shortstop&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Positioning")]

Unlike the [pitcher](/wiki/Pitcher "Pitcher") and [catcher](/wiki/Catcher "Catcher"), who must start every play in a designated area (the pitcher must be on the [pitcher's mound](/wiki/Pitcher%27s_mound "Pitcher's mound"), with one foot in contact with the [pitcher's rubber](/wiki/Pitcher%27s_rubber "Pitcher's rubber"), and the catcher must be behind [home plate](/wiki/Home_plate "Home plate") in the [catcher's box](/wiki/Catcher%27s_box "Catcher's box")) the shortstop and the other fielders can vary their positioning in response to what they anticipate will be the actions of the batter and runner(s) once the play begins.[[7]](#cite_note-7)

The shortstop ordinarily is positioned near second base on the third-base side. Because right-handed hitters tend to hit the ball more toward third base, a shortstop will generally move closer to third base if the batter is batting right-handed, and more toward first base if the batter is batting left-handed. A shortstop typically has a strong throwing arm, because he has a relatively long throw to first base, and often has less time in which to make a throw, given that the [ground balls](/wiki/Ground_ball "Ground ball") he fields have often traveled relatively far. A shortstop must also be extremely agile, because balls hit to or near the shortstop position are usually hit harder than to other infield positions.

Shortstops are required to [cover second base](/wiki/Covering_a_base "Covering a base") in [double play](/wiki/Double_play "Double play") situations when the ball is hit to the second baseman or first baseman. They also cover second when a runner is attempting a [stolen base](/wiki/Stolen_base "Stolen base"), but only when a left-handed hitter is batting because the infield will respond to a left-handed batter by shifting toward first base, resulting in the shortstop being the infielder who is closest to second base. Shortstops also must cover third at various times, including the rotation play; the latter occurs when there are runners on first and second and a [sacrifice bunt](/wiki/Sacrifice_bunt "Sacrifice bunt") is attempted toward third base, requiring the third baseman to move in away from third base in order to field it. Shortstops generally are given precedence on catching pop-ups in the [infield](/wiki/Infielder "Infielder") as well, so they end up calling off other players many times, although on deep pop-ups they generally fall back when called off by an [outfielder](/wiki/Outfielder "Outfielder"). They often become the cutoff man on balls to any part of the outfield that are being directed towards third base and all balls to left and center field that are destined for second base. Depending on the system the shortstop may cut balls from left field heading home; however, this is usually the job of the third baseman.

The emphasis on defense makes the position unusually difficult to fill. Historically, a strong shortstop did not have to be a good hitter. Some of the weakest hitters in Major League Baseball have played the position, including [Mario Mendoza](/wiki/Mario_Mendoza "Mario Mendoza"), for whom [George Brett](/wiki/George_Brett "George Brett") popularized the eponymous [Mendoza Line](/wiki/Mendoza_Line "Mendoza Line") to describe a batting average below .200. Since the 1960s, however, such mediocre hitting has become rarer as teams increasingly demand players with ability to both field and hit.[[8]](#cite_note-seminara20100706-8)

In practice, a marginal shortstop who hits well can be moved to almost any other position, especially second base or third base, whether early in their careers (examples: George Brett and [Mike Schmidt](/wiki/Mike_Schmidt "Mike Schmidt") were both tried early in their careers as shortstops)[[9]](#cite_note-9)[[10]](#cite_note-10) or later due to diminished [fielding range](/w/index.php?title=Fielding_range&action=edit&redlink=1 "Fielding range (page does not exist)"), slower reflexes, weaker throwing arms, increased risk of injury, or co-existence with another dominant shortstop, as with [Ernie Banks](/wiki/Ernie_Banks "Ernie Banks"), [Cal Ripken Jr.](/wiki/Cal_Ripken_Jr. "Cal Ripken Jr."), [Alex Rodríguez](/wiki/Alex_Rodr%C3%ADguez "Alex Rodríguez"), [Michael Young](/wiki/Michael_Young_(baseball) "Michael Young (baseball)"), or [Miguel Tejada](/wiki/Miguel_Tejada "Miguel Tejada").

## Significant shortstops

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Shortstop&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Significant shortstops")]

### Shortstops inducted to the Baseball Hall of Fame

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Shortstop&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Shortstops inducted to the Baseball Hall of Fame")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b2/Ozzie_Smith_1983.jpg/250px-Ozzie_Smith_1983.jpg)](/wiki/File:Ozzie_Smith_1983.jpg)

[Cardinals](/wiki/St._Louis_Cardinals "St. Louis Cardinals") great [Ozzie Smith](/wiki/Ozzie_Smith "Ozzie Smith")

The year in which the player was inducted is given in brackets after his name.

* [Luis Aparicio](/wiki/Luis_Aparicio "Luis Aparicio") (1984)
* [Luke Appling](/wiki/Luke_Appling "Luke Appling") (1964)
* [Dave Bancroft](/wiki/Dave_Bancroft "Dave Bancroft") (1971)
* [Ernie Banks](/wiki/Ernie_Banks "Ernie Banks") (1977)
* [Lou Boudreau](/wiki/Lou_Boudreau "Lou Boudreau") (1970)
* [Joe Cronin](/wiki/Joe_Cronin "Joe Cronin") (1956)
* [George Davis](/wiki/George_Davis_(baseball) "George Davis (baseball)") (1998)
* [Travis Jackson](/wiki/Travis_Jackson "Travis Jackson") (1982)
* [Hughie Jennings](/wiki/Hughie_Jennings "Hughie Jennings") (1945)
* [Derek Jeter](/wiki/Derek_Jeter "Derek Jeter") (2020)
* [Barry Larkin](/wiki/Barry_Larkin "Barry Larkin") (2012)
* [John Henry "Pop" Lloyd](/wiki/John_Henry_Lloyd "John Henry Lloyd") (1977)
* [Rabbit Maranville](/wiki/Rabbit_Maranville "Rabbit Maranville") (1954)
* [Paul Molitor](/wiki/Paul_Molitor "Paul Molitor") (2004) (Played some games at SS in 1978, 1979, and 1982)
* [Pee Wee Reese](/wiki/Pee_Wee_Reese "Pee Wee Reese") (1984)
* [Cal Ripken Jr.](/wiki/Cal_Ripken_Jr. "Cal Ripken Jr.") (2007)
* [Phil Rizzuto](/wiki/Phil_Rizzuto "Phil Rizzuto") (1994)
* [Joe Sewell](/wiki/Joe_Sewell "Joe Sewell") (1977)
* [Ozzie Smith](/wiki/Ozzie_Smith "Ozzie Smith") (2002)
* [Joe Tinker](/wiki/Joe_Tinker "Joe Tinker") (1946)
* [Alan Trammell](/wiki/Alan_Trammell "Alan Trammell") (2018)
* [Arky Vaughan](/wiki/Arky_Vaughan "Arky Vaughan") (1985)
* [Honus Wagner](/wiki/Honus_Wagner "Honus Wagner") (1936)
* [Bobby Wallace](/wiki/Bobby_Wallace_(baseball) "Bobby Wallace (baseball)") (1953)
* [John Montgomery Ward](/wiki/John_Montgomery_Ward "John Montgomery Ward") (1964)
* [Willie Wells](/wiki/Willie_Wells "Willie Wells") (1997)
* [George Wright](/wiki/George_Wright_(sportsman) "George Wright (sportsman)") (1937)
* [Robin Yount](/wiki/Robin_Yount "Robin Yount") (1999)

#### Notes

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Shortstop&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Notes")]

1. [John Henry Lloyd](/wiki/John_Henry_Lloyd "John Henry Lloyd") and [Willie Wells](/wiki/Willie_Wells "Willie Wells") were elected for their play in the [Negro leagues](/wiki/Negro_leagues "Negro leagues").
2. George Wright was elected as a pioneer, but also starred as a shortstop in the 1860s and 1870s.
3. Robin Yount started his career as a shortstop, and moved to the [outfield](/wiki/Outfield "Outfield") where he played his last nine seasons. (Besides winning the MVP award as a shortstop in [1982](/wiki/1982_Major_League_Baseball_season "1982 Major League Baseball season"), Yount also won the award as a [centerfielder](/wiki/Centerfielder "Centerfielder") in [1989](/wiki/1989_Major_League_Baseball_season "1989 Major League Baseball season").)
4. Ernie Banks played shortstop for the first half of his career and first base for the remainder.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e8/Jetershortstop.JPG/250px-Jetershortstop.JPG)](/wiki/File:Jetershortstop.JPG)

[Yankees](/wiki/New_York_Yankees "New York Yankees") former shortstop [Derek Jeter](/wiki/Derek_Jeter "Derek Jeter") getting ready to field his position in 2007

### All-time single season assist leaders among shortstops

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Shortstop&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: All-time single season assist leaders among shortstops")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/96/Omar_Vizquel_at_Wrigley_Field.jpg/250px-Omar_Vizquel_at_Wrigley_Field.jpg)](/wiki/File:Omar_Vizquel_at_Wrigley_Field.jpg)

[Omar Vizquel](/wiki/Omar_Vizquel "Omar Vizquel") played more games at shortstop than any other player in MLB history.

1. [Ozzie Smith](/wiki/Ozzie_Smith "Ozzie Smith"): 621 (San Diego Padres, 1980)
2. [Glenn Wright](/wiki/Glenn_Wright "Glenn Wright"): 601 (Pittsburgh Pirates, 1924)
3. [Dave Bancroft](/wiki/Dave_Bancroft "Dave Bancroft"): 598 (Philadelphia Phillies/New York Giants, 1920)
4. [Tommy Thevenow](/wiki/Tommy_Thevenow "Tommy Thevenow"): 597 (St. Louis Cardinals, 1926)
5. [Iván DeJesús](/wiki/Iv%C3%A1n_DeJes%C3%BAs "Iván DeJesús"): 595 (Chicago Cubs, 1977)
